title: How to Brief a Web Designer for Best Results
excerpt: The quality of your web designer's output is directly tied to the quality of your brief. A vague brief leads to generic results, multiple revision rounds, and wasted time. This guide shows you exactly how to write a design brief that sets your project up for success.
content:
A great brief is the foundation of a great web project. The more clearly you communicate your goals, audience, brand, and requirements, the better your designer can serve you. Here's how to write one that actually works.

What a Good Design Brief Should Cover

1. About Your Business Describe what your business does, who your customers are, and what makes you different from your competitors. Include any brand guidelines, logo files, and existing color palettes. 2. Project Goals What do you want the website to achieve? More enquiries? E-commerce sales? Brand awareness? Be specific and, where possible, quantifiable. 3. Target Audience Describe your ideal customer in detail — their age, profession, challenges, goals, and what they're looking for when they find your website. 4. Competitors and Inspiration List 2–3 competitor websites and note what they do well or poorly. Share 3–5 website examples you admire (these don't have to be in your industry) and explain what you like about them. 5. Site Structure What pages do you need? Provide a basic list: Home, About, Services, Blog, Contact. If you have a more complex structure, a rough site map helps enormously. 6. Functional Requirements Do you need e-commerce, a booking system, a blog, a contact form, social media integration, or anything else? List everything. 7. Budget and Timeline Be honest about both. A designer who knows your budget can tell you what's possible. A designer who knows your deadline can flag capacity issues early. 8. Content Status Will you be providing all copy and images? Is content already written, or does the designer need to account for copywriting support?

The Payoff

A comprehensive brief doesn't just give the designer what they need — it also forces you to clarify your own thinking. Many clients discover gaps or contradictions in their plans during the briefing process.

We Make Briefing Easy
